title: [FEATURE]: Expose KiloGateway models in vscode to github copilot
body:
### Feature hasn't been suggested before.

- [x] I have verified this feature I'm about to request hasn't been suggested before.

### Describe the enhancement you want to request

I'd love to see the kilocode extension exposing the models provided by KiloGateway to other extensions and the core chat experience in VSCode. This would enable the capability to use different extensions that might do certain things better. It also would enable a smoother transition from the built-in experience to KiloCode as one could slowly move workflows over while already switching the backend billing/inference over.

Docs:
- https://code.visualstudio.com/docs/copilot/customization/language-models#_bring-your-own-language-model-key
- [`vscode.lm.registerLanguageModelChatProvider`](https://code.visualstudio.com/api/references/vscode-api#lm)
author: siegenthalerroger

title: [FEATURE]: Reduce redundant environment_details injection on every conversation turn
body:
### Feature hasn't been suggested before.

- [x] I have verified this feature I'm about to request hasn't been suggested before.

### Describe the enhancement you want to request

### Is your feature request related to a problem?

Yes. The `environment_details` block is re-injected verbatim into every single conversation turn — both user turns and tool result turns. In a typical multi-step task requiring 3+ tool calls, this block is repeated 6-8 times with identical, unchanging content, consuming ~40-60% of total conversation tokens.

Most sections never change within a single task:
- `Current Mode` (slug, name, model, role description, custom_instructions)
- `Custom Instructions` (user rules from `.kilocode/rules-code/`)
- `Current Workspace Directory Files` (full file tree)

Only a few fields actually change between turns:
- `VSCode Visible Files`
- `VSCode Open Tabs`
- `Current Time`
- `Current Cost`

For users on token-limited plans (especially `auto-free`), this repeated injection is the single largest source of token waste.

#### Context

While investigating this, I built an experimental MCP server ([TokenGuardian](https://github.com/hienlv102/token-guardian-)) that compresses file content before returning it to the model. Even with ~25-40% content compression, the dominant source of token waste remains the repeated `environment_details` injection — which no MCP tool can address. This confirms the fix needs to happen at the host/IDE level.

### Describe the solution you'd like

**Option A — Split static vs dynamic (simplest)**

Move static sections to the system prompt (injected once per conversation):
- `Current Mode`
- `Custom Instructions`
- `Workspace Directory Files`

Keep only dynamic sections in per-turn injection:
- `VSCode Visible Files`
- `VSCode Open Tabs`
- `Current Time`
- `Current Cost`

This reduces per-turn injection from ~1,200 tokens to ~100-150 tokens.

**Option B — Hash-based deduplication**

If a section's content is identical to the previous turn, replace it with a short marker:

```
# VSCode Open Tabs (unchanged)
# Current Mode (unchanged)
# Custom Instructions (unchanged)
# Workspace Files (unchanged)
```

Each `(unchanged)` marker costs ~2 tokens vs ~200-500 tokens for the full section.

**Option C — Differential injection**

Turn 1: inject full `environment_details` as today.
Turn 2+: inject only sections that changed since the previous turn, omit unchanged sections entirely.

### Describe alternatives you've considered

- **MCP-based compression**: Built [TokenGuardian](https://github.com/hienlv102/token-guardian-) to compress file content via MCP. It helps with file content (~25-40% savings) but cannot control the host-injected `environment_details` block.
- **Shorter custom instructions**: Reducing user rules helps marginally, but `Current Mode`, `role`, and `Workspace Files` sections are controlled by Kilo Code and cannot be shortened by users.

### Additional context

**Estimated impact:**

| Metric | Typical Value |
|--------|---------------|
| `environment_details` per turn | ~800–1,500 tokens |
| Turns per multi-step task | 5–8 |
| Redundant tokens per task | ~4,000–10,000 tokens |
| Token waste as % of conversation | ~40–60% |

**Comparison of solutions:**

| Solution | Effort | Token Savings | Risk |
|----------|--------|---------------|------|
| Option A: Static/Dynamic split | Low | ~60–70% | None |
| Option B: Hash dedup | Low–Medium | ~70–80% | Minimal |
| Option C: Differential | Medium | ~80–90% | Model must understand delta format |
author: hienlv102

title: [FEATURE]:Prevent loading files over a certain size or that would overflow context window
body:
### Feature hasn't been suggested before.

- [x] I have verified this feature I'm about to request hasn't been suggested before.

### Describe the enhancement you want to request

Sometimes the model attempts to read files that are too large. For example a large image.

This file may be so large that it overflows the context window entirely, or sufficiently large to push the current context over the limit when added.

We should detect both of these scenarios:
1. If a file is over a certain size, reject loading it entirely and instead recommend using other tools to read it. (Reduce the size of the image, read it in parts or sections, etc.)
2. If the file is under the limit but reading it would put the entire context window over the limit, we should compact first automatically then read the file.

comments.body: hi @lambertjosh yes, it’s a great idea regarding the yellow outlined animation around the box. I really liked it before, as it really drew attention to the current area that needed user action. Regarding the buttons, I think the issue might be that our accent color for primary buttons is currently a rather dark shade of blue. In my new button updates, the blue will be more vibrant and should draw more attention, so that might already solve the issue. For a reference, here's a screenshot of the new buttons color And of course we could also use a bigger button size. For example, if the buttons in the screenshot are the small size (24px), we could switch to our standard size (32px).

title: Kilo Sessions are nowhere to be found
body:
### Description

New to kilo, works great. I had 3 sessions since the last 3 days i started using it. Using it over SSH on my development machine.

I come back after 3 hours, SSH into my server do `kilocode -c` but don't return to my previous session. Its a fresh session.
I check `kilo session list` - no sessions.
I check `~/.config/kilo` and there's no sessions stored.
There is no `~/.kilo` or `~/.kilocode`

They are all gone!

Maybe related to https://github.com/Kilo-Org/kilocode/issues/6594#issue-4020847270

NOTE: This is super not cool and i can't use kilo for any serious work until i know my conversations cannot be nuked like this.

### Plugins

Not sure, i think its valina kilocode cli

### Kilo version

7.0.37

### Steps to reproduce

1. Use kilo over SSH
2. Close SSH while kilo is running
3. SSH again and no sessions

Not sure if this is reproducible or exactly how because, I've been using it this way and only now did my sessions get deleted.

### Screenshot and/or share link

_No response_

### Operating System

Fedora Server 43

### Terminal

Ghostty (zsh + oh-my-zsh)

author: TurkerTunali

comments.author: TurkerTunali
comments.body:
I've asked to Kilo Code about the situation and it returned:
**Investigation Complete: Kilo Code Session Storage Issue**
Session Storage Location
Kilo Code stores sessions at:

~/.vscode-server/data/User/globalStorage/kilocode.kilo-code/sessions/
Root Cause Analysis
I found the issue - today's session (Mar 4) was never created in the sessions folder:

Current state:

Last session record: Feb 25, 2026 (timestamp: 1772038307652)
Task folder exists from: Mar 3 (019c94ea-fc74-70eb-8d4f-6ed5f07a3b5e)
No folder for today (Mar 4)
Files Found:
sessions/ee6a5c54b2632e78/session.json - Last modified Feb 25, 2026
tasks/ directory contains 22 historical task folders dating back to Feb 7
**Why This Happens**
This appears to be a VSCode Server session synchronization issue. Your setup uses VSCode Server (~/.vscode-server/), and the session creation may have failed when you started today's session.

**Solutions to Try**
Restart VSCode Server connection - This will force a new session to be created

Check VSCode's connection status - Make sure the remote connection is stable

Try a new session - Start a completely new session and check if the session.json gets updated

Backup existing sessions - If you need to preserve old sessions:

cp -r ~/.vscode-server/data/User/globalStorage/kilocode.kilo-code/sessions ~/sessions_backup
The session data from Feb 25 should still be viewable in Agent Manager - it's showing old sessions because today's session wasn't created properly.

title: [FEATURE]: Add Tokens-Per-Second (tok/s) Metric to Kilo CLI
body:
### Feature hasn't been suggested before.

- [x] I have verified this feature I'm about to request hasn't been suggested before.

### Describe the enhancement you want to request

### Summary

Kilo CLI currently does not expose a tokens-per-second (tok/s) performance metric. Latency alone is insufficient for evaluating model performance, especially when comparing different prompts, models, or hardware configurations. You can also add these details to the sidebar within the conversation.

### Proposal

Add token-level performance metrics to the CLI output:

* Input tokens
* Output tokens
* Generation time (seconds)
* Tokens per second (output_tokens / generation_time)

Optional: enable via `--metrics` or `--verbose` flag to avoid cluttering default output.

### Example Output

```
Latency: 4.21s  
Output tokens: 512  
Tokens/sec: 121.6
```

### Rationale

Exposing tok/s enables reliable benchmarking, regression testing, hardware comparison, and optimization analysis. This metric is a de facto standard in LLM tooling.

title: bug: LLM.stream small-model requests leak into subsequent agent session
body:
## Summary

When a small-model `LLM.stream` request (commit message generation, enhance prompt) is followed by an agent session prompt, the first agent request is sent to **both** the small model and the main agent model.

## Reproduction

1. Open the extension with Kilo Gateway as the provider
2. Generate **either**:
   - A commit message (via the SCM button), **or**
   - An enhanced prompt (via the ✨ sparkle button)
3. Immediately start a new chat session and send a message
4. **Observe:** The first agent request hits both the small model (used in step 2) and the main model

## Expected

The agent session's first request should only go to the main model. The small-model request from step 2 should be completely isolated.

## Affected features

- **Commit message generation** (`packages/opencode/src/commit-message/generate.ts`) — uses `LLM.stream` with `small: true`
- **Enhance prompt** (`packages/opencode/src/kilocode/enhance-prompt.ts`) — uses `LLM.stream` with `small: true`

Both follow the same pattern:
```ts
const stream = await LLM.stream({
  agent,
  model,
  small: true,
  sessionID: "commit-message", // or "enhance-prompt"
  ...
})
```

## Likely cause

Provider state or model instance caching in `Provider.defaultModel()` / `Provider.getSmallModel()` / `Provider.getLanguage()` is shared between the small-model call and the subsequent agent session. When Kilo Gateway is the provider, this shared state causes the gateway to route the first agent request to both models.

## Environment

- Provider: Kilo Gateway (`kilo`)
- Extension: VS Code (new extension, `packages/kilo-vscode`)
- CLI: `packages/opencode`

title: Feature: Energy and carbon tracking for AI inference
body:
## Context

[Neuralwatt](https://neuralwatt.com/) runs an OpenAI-compatible inference platform that includes per-request energy measurements from hardware-level monitoring. We'd like to surface that energy data in Kilo Code alongside existing cost and token metrics.

There's growing interest in energy transparency for AI inference. Chris Adams at the [Green Web Foundation](https://www.thegreenwebfoundation.org/) has started exploring [energy tracking for OpenCode](https://gist.github.com/mrchrisadams/549aa3804ea112f04a615900fe595166) as well. If energy data eventually becomes a standard field in AI SDK responses, the sniffer approach below could be replaced with a direct read.

## What the energy data looks like

Neuralwatt's API is OpenAI-compatible. Energy data appears as an SSE comment at the end of the stream (streaming) or as a top-level field (non-streaming):

**Streaming** — energy arrives as an SSE comment before `[DONE]`:
```
data: {"choices":[{"delta":{"content":"Hello"},...}]}
data: {"choices":[{"delta":{},"finish_reason":"stop",...}]}
data: {"choices":[],"usage":{"prompt_tokens":75,"completion_tokens":15,...}}
: energy {"energy_joules":74.42,"energy_kwh":2.07e-05,"avg_power_watts":324.3,"duration_seconds":0.229,"attribution_method":"prorated","attribution_ratio":1.0}
data: [DONE]
```

**Non-streaming** — energy is a top-level field on the response:
```json
{
  "choices": [...],
  "usage": {"prompt_tokens": 75, "completion_tokens": 17},
  "energy": {
    "energy_joules": 112.38,
    "energy_kwh": 3.12e-05,
    "avg_power_watts": 308.8,
    "duration_seconds": 0.364,
    "attribution_method": "prorated",
    "attribution_ratio": 1.0
  }
}
```

The SSE comment format (`: energy ...`) is invisible to standard SSE parsers, so it doesn't interfere with existing client code.

## Proposal

Add optional energy and carbon tracking to Kilo Code. When a provider includes energy data in its streaming response, display it in the task header next to cost.

The implementation uses a `TransformStream` that wraps the fetch response in `getSDK()`, inspecting SSE lines for energy data while passing all bytes through unchanged. No changes to the Vercel AI SDK are needed. Two formats are supported today (Neuralwatt SSE comments, GreenPT impact fields), and the mechanism is provider-agnostic. Non-energy providers short-circuit after a prefix check.

When no energy data is present, nothing changes in the UI.

We've also registered Neuralwatt as a provider (via `@ai-sdk/openai-compatible`) so the feature can be tested end-to-end.

## References

- [Green Web Foundation](https://www.thegreenwebfoundation.org/)
- Chris Adams' [energy tracking proposal for OpenCode](https://gist.github.com/mrchrisadams/549aa3804ea112f04a615900fe595166)
- Our [OpenCode fork](https://github.com/neuralwatt/opencode/tree/feature/energy-tracking) with a similar implementation

title: Import settings/configuration from other AI coding clients (Cursor, Copilot, etc.)
body:
## Summary
Allow users to migrate their settings and configuration from other AI coding clients (Cursor, Copilot, Claude Code, etc.) to the Kilo Code VS Code extension.

## Context
As we onboard new users coming from other AI coding tools, providing a smooth migration path by allowing them to import their existing settings would significantly reduce friction and improve adoption.

For reference, Claude has a similar feature: https://claude.com/import-memory

## Scope
- Investigate what settings/configurations can be imported from popular AI coding clients:
  - Cursor
  - GitHub Copilot
  - Claude Code
  - Others as applicable
- Implement an import mechanism (e.g., a command or onboarding step) that reads and converts settings from these tools into Kilo Code configuration
- Consider importing things like:
  - Custom instructions / rules / memory
  - Model preferences
  - Keybindings or workflow preferences
  - Any other relevant user configuration

## Motivation
Lowering the barrier to switch to Kilo Code from competing tools will help with new user acquisition and onboarding.

---

Built for [Mark](https://kilo-code.slack.com/archives/C0A4SA041DE/p1772468906615429?thread_ts=1772432111.384819&cid=C0A4SA041DE) by [Kilo for Slack](https://kilo.ai/features/slack-integration)

Currently "Auto" can pick any model including expensive ones like Opus 4.6. Request: cost-capped Auto tiers that still auto-select, but from cheaper model families.
| Tier          | Complex Tasks       | Simple Tasks    | Cost Savings |
| ------------- | ------------------- | --------------- | ------------ |
| Auto Max      | Opus 4.6 / GPT-5    | GPT-4o / Sonnet | Baseline     |
| Auto Plus     | Sonnet 4.5 / GPT-4o | Haiku / GPT-4.1 | ~50%         |
| Auto Standard | GPT-4.1 / Haiku     | Flash / Nano    | ~80%         |
| Auto Cheap    | Flash Lite / Nano   | Same            | ~95%         |
How it works: Auto still picks the right model for each task, but won't exceed the tier's model family. Complex tasks in "Auto Plus" use Sonnet instead of Opus.

Benefit: Keep automatic optimization while capping max cost per request.

title: [FEATURE]: Autopilot (Grind) mode: automatic review-fix loops for changed diff
body:
- [x] I have verified this feature I'm about to request hasn't been suggested before.

Add an **Autopilot** mode (alias: **Grind**) that behaves like `code` mode, but automatically runs iterative review/fix cycles after task completion.

### Desired behavior

When a coding task finishes in Autopilot mode:

1. Start a local code review automatically (focused on current task diff only).
2. Feed the structured review findings back to the model.
3. Instruct the model to apply fixes for all configured blocking issues.
4. Run review again.
5. Repeat until no blocking issues remain, or stop conditions are reached.

### Scope and controls

- **Review scope:** current diff only (files/lines changed by this task/session), to avoid unrelated churn.
- **Blocking severity:** user-configurable (e.g. critical only, critical+warning, all severities).
- **Loop stop policy:** user-configurable cap (default max rounds), plus an explicit unlimited option.
- **Mode naming:** Autopilot as primary name, Grind as alias.

### Why this helps

- Reduces manual review/fix back-and-forth.
- Produces cleaner diffs before human review.
- Encourages consistent quality checks while preserving user control over strictness and cost.

### Suggested acceptance criteria

- New mode available: `autopilot` (alias `grind`).
- After initial completion, system auto-enters review/fix loop without user prompting.
- Review findings are machine-readable enough for deterministic follow-up fixing.
- Severity threshold is user-configurable.
- Max iterations is user-configurable, including unlimited mode.
- Loop only reviews current task diff and avoids broad repo-wide rewrites.
- Loop exits cleanly when blocking findings are empty or iteration limit is reached.
- Final assistant output includes:
  - total iterations run
  - issues found/fixed per round
  - unresolved items (if any)
- Telemetry/eventing added for loop start, per-round outcome, and stop reason.
- Safe interrupt/cancel support at any loop stage.

### Implementation notes (for maintainers)

A likely integration point is the existing post-completion branch in `packages/opencode/src/session/prompt.ts` where plan follow-up is currently triggered. Autopilot can reuse this pattern to inject synthetic follow-up prompts for review and fixes, using existing local review generation in `packages/opencode/src/kilocode/review/review.ts`.

title: Unexpected API Response Error when using custom OpenAI /responses endpoint
body:
Provider: openai-compatible (responses)
Model: gpt-5.3-codex

### Description

I am encountering an intermittent and irregular error when using **Kilo Code** in VS Code with a custom provider configured to use the OpenAI `/responses` endpoint via a `cli-proxy-api` proxy (proxying multiple Codex Auth accounts).

The error frequently interrupts the Agent process with the following message:

> **Unexpected API Response: The language model did not provide any assistant messages.**

## **Observed Behavior**

* **Endpoint:** `openai/responses`
* **Frequency:** Random/Stochastic. It occurs at irregular intervals during the Agent's execution.
* **Comparison:** When I switch the custom provider configuration to use the OpenAI `/chat/completions` compatibility interface, the issue **does not occur**, and the Agent works stably.

## **Expected Behavior**

The `/responses` endpoint should return valid assistant messages consistently, matching the stability of the `/chat/completions` endpoint.

## **Additional Context**

It seems like the `/responses` streaming or message structure might be handled differently by the proxy or the extension, leading to "empty" responses being perceived by the client even when the upstream provider is active.

To further isolate the issue, I conducted the following test:

* **Test Case:** Overwriting the same `cli-proxy-api` proxy configuration into the **OpenAI Codex CLI**.
* **Result:** The CLI operates **persistently and stably** without any interruptions or "empty message" errors.
* **Conclusion:** This suggests that the backend proxy service and the underlying Codex Auth accounts are stable. The issue seems specifically related to how **Kilo Code** (VS Code extension) handles the `/responses` endpoint or parses its streaming output compared to the `/chat/completions` compatibility layer.

### Plugins

vs code

### Kilo version

5.9

### Steps to reproduce

1. Configure a custom provider in Kilo Code using the `/responses` endpoint.
2. Set the Base URL to the `cli-proxy-api` address. (or any other providers using `/responses`)
3. Start an Agent task that requires multiple steps or long-running context.
4. The process eventually fails with "The language model did not provide any assistant messages."

### Screenshot and/or share link

_No response_

### Operating System

Windows 11

### Terminal

Windows power shell

title: Kilo Code indexing completes without indexing any files
body: ### Description **Summary** Kilo Code indexing completes without indexing any files **Environment** - OS: OpenSUSE 15.6 (virtual machine) - Kilo Code 5.9.0 - VS Code 1.109.5 - Qdrant 1.14 - OpenAI API, model: text-embedding-3-small (vector size 1536) - Workspace: 7 test files (.pro, .docx, .jpg, files without extension) - Git 2.43.0 **Description** After starting indexing, status shows "Indexed - File watcher started". Qdrant contains only 1 point, which is a metadata record (type: metadata, indexing_complete: true). No actual file content was embedded. 0 of 7 workspace files were indexed. **Log Analysis** _First run (initial indexing):_ [QdrantVectorStore] Warning during getCollectionInfo for "ws-e17f698ff4f7f11f". Collection may not exist or another error occurred: Not Found [QdrantVectorStore] Marked indexing as incomplete (in progress) [QdrantVectorStore] Marked indexing as complete No file processing, no embedding calls, no errors between "in progress" and "complete". The indexer jumps directly to completion without processing any files. _Second run (incremental scan):_ [CodeIndexOrchestrator] Collection already has indexed data. Running incremental scan for new/changed files... [CodeIndexOrchestrator] No new or changed files found [QdrantVectorStore] Marked indexing as complete The incremental scan believes all files are already indexed, despite no actual file embeddings existing in Qdrant. **Additional observation** - Api key is used with unsecure connection. - Qdrant connection uses HTTP instead of HTTPS. Unclear if this affects indexing. **Expected Behavior** At least text-based files (.pro, .docx) should be indexed as vector points in Qdrant. ### Plugins idl.idl-for-vscode@5.1.0, kilocode.kilo-code@5.9.0, pdconsec.vscode-print@1.6.0 ### Kilo version 5.9.0 ### Steps to reproduce see above ### Screenshot and/or share link ### Operating System OpenSUSE 15.6 as a virtual machine under Windows 10 using VirtualBox ### Terminal /bin/bash in xfce4-terminal 1.1.3

title: MCP settings not migrated after upgrading to v7.0.33 - old mcp_settings.json not read
body:
After upgrading Kilo Code to version 7.0.33, all MCP server configurations are lost. The extension no longer reads the old `mcp_settings.json` file located at:

```
%APPDATA%/Code/User/globalStorage/kilo-code.kilo-code/settings/mcp_settings.json
```

## Expected behavior
- Old MCP settings should be automatically migrated to new format, OR
- Old settings file should continue to work

## Actual behavior
- No MCP servers are loaded after upgrade
- User has to manually recreate all MCP configurations in new CLI format

## Root cause
Kilo Code v7.0.33 uses new CLI-based configuration format (`kilo.jsonc` in `~/.config/kilo/`) instead of the old VS Code extension format (`mcp_settings.json` in globalStorage). The old settings file is ignored.

## Environment
- Kilo Code version: 7.0.33
- VS Code version: Latest
- OS: Windows

## Workaround (for users)
Manually convert old `mcp_settings.json` format to new `kilo.jsonc` format:
- Change `mcpServers` to `mcp`
- Change `command` + `args` to `command` array
- Change `env` to `environment`
- Change `disabled: false` to `enabled: true`

## Suggested fix
Add automatic migration of old MCP settings when Kilo Code v7.0.33+ starts for the first time. Read old `mcp_settings.json` from globalStorage and convert to new `kilo.jsonc` format automatically.
author: metapeka

comments.author: metapeka
comments.body:
## Additional Information: API Provider Credentials Also Lost

After further investigation, this issue affects more than just MCP settings:

### Issue 2: API provider credentials lost
All API keys and provider configurations stored in VS Code Secret Storage are no longer accessible. According to Kilo Code documentation:
- "All API keys use VS Code's Secret Storage—never stored in plain text"
- "OAuth tokens can't be exported with settings"

This means users must re-enter all API keys for providers (Anthropic, OpenAI, Google, etc.) after upgrading.

## Updated Expected Behavior:
- Old MCP settings should be automatically migrated to new format, OR
- Old settings file should continue to work
- API provider credentials should persist across upgrades (or be exportable)

## Updated Workaround:
1. Manually convert old `mcp_settings.json` to new `kilo.jsonc` format
2. Re-enter all API provider credentials in Settings → Providers
3. After reconfiguring, use Export (Settings → bottom of page) to create a backup

## Updated Suggested Fixes:
1. Add automatic migration of old MCP settings when Kilo Code v7.0.33+ starts for the first time
2. Add support for exporting Secret Storage credentials in the settings export file (with user warning about security)
3. Or maintain backward compatibility with old `mcp_settings.json` format

comments.author: metapeka
comments.body:
**Update: Marketplace is Not Implemented**

After further investigation, I found that the Marketplace feature is actually **not implemented yet** in Kilo Code v7.0.33:

From the source code documentation (`packages/kilo-vscode/docs/non-agent-features/marketplace.md`):

```
# Marketplace (Installable Modes, MCP Servers, Skills)

**Priority:** P2
**Status:** ❌ Not started

## What Exists
- Toolbar button renders `<DummyView title="Marketplace" />` — a placeholder with no functionality

## Remaining Work
- Catalog browsing (Modes, MCP servers, Skills)
- Install/uninstall/update capabilities
- Marketplace API endpoints
- Search and filtering within the catalog
```

This means:
- The Marketplace tab showing empty is **expected behavior** - the feature is not built yet
- Skills, MCP servers, and Modes must be installed manually
- This is not a bug from the upgrade - it's a missing feature

**Suggestion:** Please implement the Marketplace functionality or provide clear documentation on how to manually install Skills/MCPs/Modes from the kilo-marketplace repository.

Since CLI configs are now in `~/.config/kilo` (see comment on #5674), it's rather unintuitive that a `custom_modes.yaml` placed in that directory is ignored, The expected global modes location is `~/.kilocode/cli/global/settings/custom_modes.yaml`, even though the base `~/.kilocode` folder is never created with the new CLI.

This path is also not documented in the [page about custom modes](https://kilo.ai/docs/customize/custom-modes).

Either custom modes should also be loaded from `~/.config/kilo`, or this behavior should be explicitly documented on the relevant page with expected paths.

PR: #6489

title: [CLI] Memory grows unbounded during normal sessions — bmalloc fragmentation traps freed pages
body:
### Description

A single Kilo CLI session accumulates physical memory that is never released back to the OS. GC reclaims objects successfully (resident drops), but bmalloc/libpas never returns the freed pages (footprint and dirty pages keep climbing).

**The killer datapoint:** after 4.5 hours idle with no active work:

```
Physical footprint:   8.1 GB
Resident (live):     85.7 MB
Dirty (trapped):      6.8 GB
Regions:              325 (zero released)
```

85 MB of live data. 6.8 GB of empty pages bmalloc refuses to release. **80:1 waste ratio.**

`--resume` on the same session data: 692 MB, 0 B dirty, 59 regions. Same data, fresh allocator.

### Environment

- **Kilo CLI:** built from source, main branch
- **Bun:** 1.3.9
- **OS:** macOS 15.x (Darwin arm64, Apple Silicon)
- **RAM:** 24 GB unified memory
- **Build flags:** `--smol` baked in binary (`build.ts:177`)
- **Env:** `BUN_GARBAGE_COLLECTOR_LEVEL=2`

### Reproduction

1. Start a fresh `kilo` session
2. Record baseline: `vmmap -summary <PID> | grep -E "Physical footprint|WebKit Malloc"`
3. Run a normal workload: several Task subagents (e.g., 3 parallel code reviews), followed by main agent tool calls (file reads, writes, test execution, compaction)
4. Re-check vmmap periodically
5. After work completes, leave the session idle for 30+ minutes and check again

**Expected:** footprint returns to near-baseline after GC reclaims objects.
**Actual:** footprint never recovers. Freed pages are trapped inside bmalloc regions as dirty pages. Even after hours of idle, the scavenger cannot release them.

### Two Independent Growth Patterns

27 vmmap snapshots across 2 sessions revealed two distinct patterns:

#### Pattern A — Subagent heap churn (MITIGATED by `--smol`)

With `--smol` enabled, subagent tool calls produce zero footprint growth. During 55+ tool calls across 3 parallel reviewers, footprint stayed flat at 1.4 GB with 97 regions and 0 B dirty.

**Why `--smol` works here:** subagent allocations are small and short-lived (individual tool call buffers). JSC's aggressive GC in `--smol` mode frees them before regions fragment.

#### Pattern B — Main process accumulation (UNSOLVED, dominant)

Main agent tool calls cause steady, unbounded growth regardless of `--smol`, `BUN_GARBAGE_COLLECTOR_LEVEL=2`, or explicit `Bun.gc(true)`. Temporary dips from GC never recover to previous baselines.

| Pattern | Cause | `--smol` effect |
|---------|-------|-----------------|
| A (subagent) | Small, short-lived allocations | **Mitigated** |
| B (main) | Large, overlapping history copies per LLM round | **No effect** |

### Evidence: Fragmentation vs. Live Objects

Three consecutive snapshots during main agent file writes show the signature clearly:

```
S19:  footprint 3.7 GB  |  resident 4.7 GB  |  dirty   99 MB  |  regions 216
S20:  footprint 5.5 GB  |  resident 4.7 GB  |  dirty  854 MB  |  regions 251
S21:  footprint 6.1 GB  |  resident 4.4 GB  |  dirty  1.2 GB  |  regions 258
```

- **Resident drops** (4.7 → 4.4 GB): GC is freeing objects successfully
- **Footprint rises** (3.7 → 6.1 GB): OS allocation grows despite GC
- **Dirty rises** (99 MB → 1.2 GB): freed pages remain mapped but unused

This is the textbook signature of allocator-level fragmentation in a non-moving allocator.

### Root Cause: Allocation Pipeline

The dominant allocation source is the per-round message lifecycle. Every LLM round:

1. **Full DB re-hydration:** `MessageV2.stream(sessionID)` loads ALL messages + ALL parts from SQLite (including compacted tool outputs whose payloads were never cleared — `compaction.ts` sets `time.compacted` but leaves `state.output` intact)
2. **Full conversion:** `toModelMessages()` builds a fresh array from internal format (`message-v2.ts:502`)
3. **AI SDK conversion:** `convertToModelMessages()` builds another fresh array
4. **Provider transform:** potential additional full-array copy
5. **Background scans:** `summarize()` and `prune()` each load full session history again, fire-and-forget

At peak: 3-4 copies of the full message history coexist in the heap. GC eventually frees old copies, but pages are already fragmented — live objects from overlapping allocation waves are interleaved across regions, preventing bmalloc from releasing any complete page.

### Comparison: Gemini CLI's Solution

[Gemini CLI](https://github.com/google-gemini/gemini-cli) (Google's public CLI agent) solves this same problem architecturally with two layered defenses:

1. **Tool Output Masking** ([`toolOutputMaskingService.ts`](https://github.com/google-gemini/gemini-cli/blob/main/packages/core/src/services/toolOutputMaskingService.ts)): backward scan replaces old tool outputs with compact previews (head/tail excerpts) + temp file references. A protection window preserves the newest ~50K tokens; older outputs are masked to small stubs. This bounds total tool output token weight in the conversation history.

2. **Chat Compression** ([`chatCompressionService.ts`](https://github.com/google-gemini/gemini-cli/blob/main/packages/core/src/services/chatCompressionService.ts)): when history exceeds 50% of context window, the oldest 70% is LLM-summarized and the entire chat object is *replaced* (`this.chat = startChat(newHistory)`). This makes all old history objects collectible simultaneously — the only pattern that works against a non-moving allocator.

The design principle: **the heap holds at most one context window's worth of data.** Per-round allocation is bounded by context window size, not session history size. Fragmentation never reaches critical mass because the live-data surface stays small.

### What I've Tried

| Approach | Result |
|----------|--------|
| `--smol` | Mitigates Pattern A (subagents). No effect on Pattern B (main) |
| `BUN_GARBAGE_COLLECTOR_LEVEL=2` | No measurable footprint reduction |
| `Bun.gc(true)` after subagent completion | Resident freed, footprint unchanged |
| `malloc_zone_pressure_relief(NULL, 0)` via FFI | No effect (bmalloc bypasses libmalloc) |
| `--resume` (restart process) | Full resolution — 692 MB, 0 B dirty |
| 4.5 hours idle | Scavenger cannot release fragmented pages |

### Proposed Fix: Three Layered Defenses

Inspired by the Gemini CLI architecture, adapted to Kilo's SQLite-backed message model:

#### Layer 1: Clear compacted tool outputs in DB (highest impact, smallest change)

When compaction marks a tool part (`time.compacted`), also clear `state.output` in the database. Currently, compaction replaces outputs with placeholders in the *prompt* but leaves full payloads in SQLite — so every future round re-hydrates them.

This addresses the dominant allocation source. For a session with 200 tool calls averaging 50KB each, per-round re-hydration drops from ~10 MB to ~200 KB.

**Touches:** `compaction.ts` + guards at 3 call sites that read `state.output` after compaction (`acp/agent.ts:369,884`, `session-context-breakdown.ts:30`).

#### Layer 2: Bounded history loading from SQLite

Instead of loading ALL messages every round, load only the messages that fit the context window. Newest-first with token budget. Background scans (`summarize`, `prune`) should use the same bounded loading.

**Touches:** `message-v2.ts`, `prompt.ts`, `summary.ts`, `compaction.ts`.

#### Layer 3: Circuit breaker restart

After turn close, if `process.memoryUsage()` exceeds threshold (~4 GB from our data), gracefully re-exec with `--resume`. This is the only mechanism that resets bmalloc state, and `--resume` is already proven to work.

**Touches:** `prompt.ts` (~20 lines).

### Why This Is Not a Bun Bug

bmalloc/libpas is working as designed — it's a non-moving allocator optimized for browser tab lifecycle (short-lived processes). Its scavenger only releases completely empty pages. It cannot compact live allocations. This is architecturally incompatible with a long-running process that repeatedly allocates and frees large overlapping object graphs.

The fix is not to change the allocator — it's to stop creating the allocation pattern the allocator cannot handle. Bound per-round allocations by context window size, and the fragmentation surface stays small enough that it never matters.

<details>
<summary>Full vmmap profiling data (27 snapshots across 2 sessions)</summary>

### Session 1 — PID 21535 (`--smol` + `BUN_GARBAGE_COLLECTOR_LEVEL=2`)

| # | Phase | Footprint | WK resident | WK regions | Dirty | Notes |
|---|-------|-----------|-------------|------------|-------|-------|
| 0 | post-review launch | 1.1 GB | 889 MB | 82 | 64.6 MB | |
| 1 | 2/3 reviews completed | 1.1 GB | 805 MB | 91 | 95.2 MB | resident decreased — `--smol` releasing |
| 2 | all reviews done, main active | 1.5 GB | 1.2 GB | 101 | 147.2 MB | |
| 3 | Prometheus active (53 calls) | 1.3 GB | 951 MB | 101 | 112.6 MB | dropped during subagent |
| 4 | Prometheus done, main active | 4.2 GB | 1.5 GB | 159 | 1.6 GB | main ingesting output |
| 5 | main direct tool calls | 5.4 GB | 2.3 GB | 211 | — | monotonic growth |
| 6 | compaction | 6.3 GB | 1.2 GB | 248 | — | resident -1.1 GB, footprint +0.9 GB |
| 7 | post-compact resumed | 7.1 GB | 3.0 GB | 261 | — | refills immediately |
| 8 | running pytest (679 tests) | 10.5 GB | 3.9 GB | 381 | — | |
| 9 | post-test idle | 11.3 GB | 1.9 GB | 413 | 8.3 GB | resident -2 GB, dirty 8.3 GB |

### Resume → Session 2 — PID 17492 (same data, fresh process)

| # | Phase | Footprint | WK resident | WK regions | Dirty | Notes |
|---|-------|-----------|-------------|------------|-------|-------|
| 10 | **fresh resume baseline** | **692 MB** | **650 MB** | **59** | **0 B** | 16:1 ratio from S9 |
| 11 | tripartite review active | 1.4 GB | 1.9 GB | 97 | 0 B | |
| 12 | 1 review done, 2 active | 1.4 GB | 1.9 GB | 97 | 0 B | **flat — `--smol` works on subagents** |
| 13 | all reviews done, main active | 1.4 GB | 2.2 GB | 106 | 0 B | |
| 14 | main writing + testing | 1.5 GB | 2.4 GB | 114 | 0 B | |
| 15 | main absorbed test output | 1.7 GB | 2.7 GB | 128 | 0 B | Pattern B visible |
| 16 | compacting | 1.6 GB | 2.7 GB | 128 | 0 B | |
| 17 | post-compact resumed | 1.9 GB | 3.0 GB | 138 | 0 B | |
| 18 | main writing files | 2.3 GB | 3.5 GB | 159 | 0 B | accelerating |
| 19 | main writing | 3.7 GB | 4.7 GB | 216 | 98.9 MB | dirty appeared |
| 20 | +3 min | 5.5 GB | 4.7 GB | 251 | 854 MB | fragmentation accelerating |
| 21 | +2 min | 6.1 GB | 4.4 GB | 258 | 1.2 GB | resident down, footprint up |
| 22 | main reading + writing | 7.2 GB | 4.8 GB | 289 | 1.7 GB | |
| 23 | main idle | 8.1 GB | 5.2 GB | 325 | 2.4 GB | idle, still growing |
| 24 | idle +3 min | 7.8 GB | 4.2 GB | 325 | 2.8 GB | GC freed 1 GB, dirty grew |
| 25 | idle +30 min | 8.1 GB | 1.9 GB | 325 | 5.2 GB | more GC, more dirty |
| 26 | **idle 4.5 hours** | **8.1 GB** | **85.7 MB** | **325** | **6.8 GB** | **85 MB live, 6.8 GB trapped** |

</details>

### Related

- PR #6236 (`fix/memory-leak`) addresses MCP child process orphaning — a different issue
- Bun issues [#24386](https://github.com/oven-sh/bun/issues/24386), [#23553](https://github.com/oven-sh/bun/issues/23553), [#22530](https://github.com/oven-sh/bun/issues/22530) — upstream reports of bmalloc memory not being released

I'm happy to contribute a PR for any of the proposed fix layers. Layer 1 (clear compacted tool outputs) is the smallest change with the highest impact and could be a standalone PR.
author: marlian

comments.author: peterkc
comments.body:
@marlian analysis is accurate — here's the evidence trail and one edge case worth considering.

## Verification of Core Claims

Five claims checked, all confirmed:

| Claim | Verdict | Code Reference |
|-------|---------|---------------|
| `prune()` sets `time.compacted`, leaves `state.output` in DB | Confirmed | [`compaction.ts:93-94`](https://github.com/Kilo-Org/kilocode/blob/main/packages/opencode/src/session/compaction.ts#L93) — stamps timestamp, writes full part back via `updatePart` |
| `stream()` loads all messages + all parts | Confirmed | [`message-v2.ts:727-778`](https://github.com/Kilo-Org/kilocode/blob/main/packages/opencode/src/session/message-v2.ts#L727) — pages in batches of 50 but exhausts all rows |
| `toModelMessages()` builds a fresh array | Confirmed | [`message-v2.ts:502`](https://github.com/Kilo-Org/kilocode/blob/main/packages/opencode/src/session/message-v2.ts#L502) — fresh `UIMessage[]`, new objects per part |
| `summarize()` loads full unfiltered history | Confirmed | [`summary.ts:75`](https://github.com/Kilo-Org/kilocode/blob/main/packages/opencode/src/session/summary.ts#L75) — `Session.messages()` with no compaction filter |
| `prune()` loads full unfiltered history | Confirmed | [`compaction.ts:62`](https://github.com/Kilo-Org/kilocode/blob/main/packages/opencode/src/session/compaction.ts#L62) — same pattern |

## One Nuance on the Allocation Pipeline

The report describes `prompt.ts` as performing "full DB re-hydration" every round. There's a subtlety: [`prompt.ts:337`](https://github.com/Kilo-Org/kilocode/blob/main/packages/opencode/src/session/prompt.ts#L337) uses `MessageV2.filterCompacted(MessageV2.stream(sessionID))`, which truncates at the compaction boundary — it doesn't send all messages to the model.

But the fragmentation argument still holds: `stream()` loads every part's `state.output` from SQLite into the JS heap *before* `filterCompacted` discards the old ones. The allocation damage happens at hydration time, not at the prompt-building step. The substitution at [`message-v2.ts:631`](https://github.com/Kilo-Org/kilocode/blob/main/packages/opencode/src/session/message-v2.ts#L631) (`part.state.time.compacted ? "[Old tool result content cleared]" : part.state.output`) replaces the string *after* it was already allocated.

So the per-round allocation surface is smaller than "full history" but larger than "post-compaction window" — and `summarize()` + `prune()` do load unfiltered, as described.

## Edge Case for Layer 1: Revert Interaction

Worth considering before implementing Layer 1 (clearing `state.output` on compaction):

The revert system ([`revert.ts`](https://github.com/Kilo-Org/kilocode/blob/main/packages/opencode/src/session/revert.ts)) works at the message/part level — it deletes entire rows, not individual fields. So clearing `state.output` wouldn't break the revert delete path.

However, `unrevert()` ([`revert.ts:82`](https://github.com/Kilo-Org/kilocode/blob/main/packages/opencode/src/session/revert.ts#L82)) restores a git snapshot but doesn't restore message field values. If compaction runs and clears `state.output`, then the user reverts to before that compaction point, the tool outputs would be permanently lost — no path to recover them. The current behavior (output preserved in DB, just masked with a placeholder string in the prompt) is accidentally recoverable.

This probably doesn't matter in practice — compacted outputs are old enough that recovering them has minimal value. But it's a behavioral change worth documenting.

## Summary

The analysis is thorough and accurate. The proposed Layer 1 fix (clear `state.output` in DB during compaction) would directly address the dominant allocation source with a small surface area. The Gemini CLI comparison is also apt — bounded per-round allocation is the right architectural direction for a long-running process on a non-moving allocator.

comments.author: marlian
comments.body:
Thanks for the thorough verification, @peterkc — checking all five claims against the actual code is exactly the kind of rigor that makes these discussions productive.

**On the `filterCompacted` nuance:** good catch, and I should have been more precise. The issue description overstates the per-round allocation surface by attributing "full DB re-hydration" to `prompt.ts` directly. As you point out, the prompt path truncates at the compaction boundary — it's `stream()` that does the damage by hydrating every `state.output` before `filterCompacted` discards the old ones. The fragmentation argument holds, but the allocation pipeline description in the issue was imprecise. I'll update the issue body to reflect this.

**On the revert edge case:** agreed this is a real behavioral change worth documenting. The current "accidentally recoverable" property is a nice side effect of the current design, even if no one is relying on it intentionally.

This got me thinking about a different angle. Kilo already has a session fork mechanism (`Session.fork` in `session/index.ts`) that clones messages up to a cutoff point and supports the compaction boundary — `filterCompacted` correctly recognizes the summary marker in forked sessions. What if compaction *forked* to a new session instead of pruning in-place?

The flow would be:
1. Compaction generates the summary (as today)
2. Fork from the compaction boundary forward → new session with only post-compaction context
3. Old session stays intact as a read-only archive — full revert fidelity, zero data loss
4. Process can re-exec on the new session → fresh allocator state

This would sidestep the revert trade-off entirely — no need to choose between clearing `state.output` for memory and preserving it for safety. And it turns Layer 3 (circuit breaker restart) from a memory-threshold workaround into a structural property of compaction.

The pieces are mostly there — `Session.fork` already handles message cloning with ID remapping and cutoff support. The main gap is that forked sessions don't set `parent_id` (they're born as root sessions), so there's no lineage tracking yet. But that's a small addition.

Just brainstorming at this point — not sure if the UX implications (switching session IDs mid-conversation) are acceptable. But the machinery is surprisingly close to supporting this already.

title: [FEATURE]: Keep latest prompt and agent response when doing context condensing
body:
### Feature hasn't been suggested before.

- [x] I have verified this feature I'm about to request hasn't been suggested before.

### Describe the enhancement you want to request

Context condensing completely removes all context including the last prompt and agent response. This last part is vital for proper operation and often I see that the agent does the task with which this the chat thread was initiated with. It sees it as a task and does it again even-though we had moved on from it long time ago.

Iterative operations tend to get hit very bad as one iteration feeds into the next.

**Request:** Let's keep the last prompt and response. We don't need to keep the agentic/operation history in-between the last prompt and response. Currently, the workaround is to not have instructions or tasks in the initial prompt in a new task/chat thread. (it's better but definitely lacks continuity.)

comments.author: 1bcMax
comments.body:
Tangential approach: instead of making it easier to manually pick models, automate the selection.

[ClawRouter](https://github.com/BlockRunAI/ClawRouter) classifies each prompt across 15 dimensions and routes to the cheapest capable model. Simple prompts → Haiku/Flash, complex → Opus/GPT-4o. Users stop thinking about which model to pick.

<1ms local classification, no API calls for routing. Open source (MIT).

Disclosure: I work on ClawRouter.

comments.author: shssoichiro
comments.body: Kilo already has the ability for Auto model selection. Some users such as myself prefer more control over the model selection. Having the option for both approaches is a good thing, in my opinion.

title: Improve mode switching UX: nicer prompts and auto-switch capability
body:
## Description

When using the new VSCode extension, the mode switching experience is lacking compared to previous behavior. Specifically:

1. **Nicer mode switch prompting** — Previously there was a smoother, more intuitive way to prompt the user to switch modes (e.g., from Ask to Code mode). Currently, when a user asks the agent to switch modes and implement something, the agent redundantly asks "do you want to switch to code mode and implement this?" instead of just popping up a prompt that the user can agree to.

2. **Auto-switch capability** — The ability to automatically switch modes based on context (e.g., auto-switching from Ask/Plan mode to Code mode when implementation is requested) should be a supported permission.

## Context

- Reported in Slack #new-vscode-feedback thread
- Related PR for planning suggestions: https://github.com/Kilo-Org/kilocode/pull/6240
- Current behavior: user in Ask mode asks to implement something → agent asks confirmation to switch instead of auto-switching or smoothly transitioning

---

Built for [josh](https://kilo-code.slack.com/archives/C0AFBRXUH2N/p1772031884565489?thread_ts=1771968053.114589&cid=C0AFBRXUH2N) by [Kilo for Slack](https://kilo.ai/features/slack-integration)

title: GPT-5 context limit should be 272k, not 400k
body:
## Description

The `limit.context` value for GPT-5 models appears to be set to 400,000 tokens, but this is the combined marketing figure (context + max output).

## Technical Details

From `models-snapshot.ts`:
```json
"limit":{"context":400000,"output":128000}
```

However, OpenAI's 400k figure is:
- 272k context window
- 128k max output

The percentage calculation in `sidebar.tsx` divides total tokens by `limit.context`, which means users see ~50% usage when they're actually at ~75% of the real context window.

## Suggested Fix

```json
"limit":{"context":272000,"output":128000}
```

This applies to all GPT-5 variants (gpt-5, gpt-5-thinking, gpt-5-codex, etc.).

## Impact

- Users get incorrect context saturation percentage
- Could lead to unexpected context truncation
author: marlian

comments.author: peterkc
comments.body:
I investigated this and believe the root cause may be different from what's described. The data appears to be correct — the bug is in the UI consumers that read it.

## The data is already right

`models.dev` (and the generated snapshot) reports three distinct fields for GPT-5 models:

```json
"limit": { "context": 400000, "input": 272000, "output": 128000 }
```

These map to OpenAI's actual architecture:

```
  |<------------ 400k total context window ------------>|
  |                                                      |
  |<---- 272k max input ---->|<---- 128k max output --->|
  |                           |                          |
  limit.context = 400000      |   limit.output = 128000
  limit.input   = 272000 -----+
```

All three values are correct. The 400k figure isn't a "marketing number" — it's the real total envelope. OpenAI enforces 272k on input because `272k + 128k = 400k`.

## What the sidebar currently shows vs. what it should

```
  CURRENT (sidebar.tsx:59)
  Uses limit.context (400k) as denominator

  User at 200k total tokens:
    200k / 400k = 50%  <-- looks fine, but...
    200k / 272k = 74%  <-- actually near the input wall

  CORRECT
  Uses limit.input (272k) as denominator

  User at 200k total tokens:
    200k / 272k = 74%  <-- reflects real proximity to rejection
```

The same issue applies to the VSCode consumer in `session-utils.ts` (line 62).

## Compaction already handles this correctly

`compaction.ts` (lines 44-46) uses `limit.input` when available:

```ts
const usable = input.model.limit.input
  ? input.model.limit.input - reserved
  : context - ProviderTransform.maxOutputTokens(input.model)
```

This correctly triggers compaction against the 272k input limit. No changes needed here.

## Suggested fix

Change the denominator in UI percentage calculations to prefer `limit.input`:

```ts
// sidebar.tsx (line 59) and session-utils.ts (line 62)
// Before:
model.limit.context

// After:
model.limit.input ?? model.limit.context
```

The `?? limit.context` fallback preserves correct behavior for models that don't have a separate `limit.input` field (e.g., Claude, Gemini, older OpenAI models where input ≈ context).

### Affected consumers

| File | Line | Status |
|------|------|--------|
| `sidebar.tsx` | 59 | Uses `limit.context` — needs fix |
| `session-utils.ts` | 62 | Uses `limit.context` — needs fix |
| `session-context-breakdown.ts` | caller-dependent | Verify what the callsite passes as `input` |
| `compaction.ts` | 44 | Already uses `limit.input` — no change needed |

### Scope

This affects any model where `limit.input < limit.context`. Currently that's all GPT-5 variants (gpt-5, gpt-5-mini, gpt-5-nano, gpt-5-codex, gpt-5.1-*, gpt-5.2-*). Models without a separate `limit.input` are unaffected by the fix since the `??` fallback preserves current behavior.

### Why changing limit.context to 272k would be the wrong fix

Setting `limit.context = 272000` (as attempted in one fork) would corrupt downstream logic:

```
  compaction fallback when limit.input is missing:
    usable = context - maxOutputTokens
           = 272k   - 128k
           = 144k   <-- WRONG (should be 272k)
```

It would also disagree with models.dev and get overwritten on the next build (since the snapshot is auto-generated from models.dev at build time).

### Prior art

For reference, [Aider takes a similar approach](https://github.com/Aider-AI/aider/blob/main/aider/coders/base_coder.py) — it uses `max_input_tokens` from litellm as its denominator rather than the total context window.

title: [FEATURE]: Multi-Tool Execution Plan Before API Calls
body:
### Feature hasn't been suggested before.

- [x] I have verified this feature I'm about to request hasn't been suggested before.

### Describe the enhancement you want to request

# 1000+ API Requests, Half a Day of Work, and 33 Million Tokens — Yet a Primitive Task Remains Unresolved

## Problem Description

### Task Context
I am migrating from PyCharm to VS Code and wanted to port the open source `Nightfall` theme from PyCharm to VS Code. 

The task seemed straightforward:
1. Take a JSON color file from a GitHub repository
2. Map colors to VS Code settings on themes.vscode.one website
3. Generate a theme file for VS Code

### What Happened in Practice
Instead of analyzing the task and executing a batch of actions in one flow:
```
request → click → click → click → write
```

The system sent a separate API request to the model for **every single action**:
```
request → analysis → click → request → analysis → click → request → analysis → click → request → write
```

### Result
- **1000+ API requests** to the model
- **33 million tokens** consumed
- **Half a day of work**
- **Task still not resolved**

## Root Cause

Kilo Code references a system prompt with a critical limitation:

> You must use exactly one tool call per assistant response. Do not call zero tools or more than one tool in the same response.

This limitation forces the system to:
1. Execute only one tool at a time
2. Send the full context (system instructions ~15-20K tokens, dialogue history, environment details) with every request
3. Wait for the model's response before each subsequent action

### Current Behavior Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[User Request] --> B[API call with full context]
    B --> C[Model returns 1 tool call]
    C --> D[Tool execution]
    D --> E[Result added to history]
    E --> F[New API call with even larger context]
    F --> G[Model returns 1 tool call]
    G --> H[Tool execution]
    H --> I[And so on...]
```

## Expected Behavior

A critical logic should be implemented following this principle:

### Step 1: Analysis and Planning
```
Model analyzes the task and creates a list of tool calls:
- read_file: colors.json
- browser_navigate: themes.vscode.one
- browser_click: selector-1
- browser_click: selector-2
- browser_snapshot: get colors
- write_file: theme.json
```

### Step 2: Execute Tool List
```
System executes tools sequentially (without parallel processing, but without intermediate model requests):
- read_file → result saved
- browser_navigate → result saved
- browser_click → result saved
- browser_click → result saved
- browser_snapshot → result saved
- write_file → result saved
```

### Step 3: Send Results to Model
```
All results are sent to the model in a single request for analysis and forming the next action plan
```

### Expected Behavior Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[User Request] --> B[API call: create a plan]
    B --> C[Model returns list of tools]
    C --> D[Execute tool 1]
    D --> E[Execute tool 2]
    E --> F[Execute tool N]
    F --> G[API call with all tool results]
    G --> H[Model analyzes and returns response or next plan]
```

## Benefits of Proposed Approach

| Metric | Current Behavior | Expected Behavior |
|---------|------------------|-------------------|
| API requests | 1000+ | ~10-50 |
| Tokens | 33 million | ~1-3 million |
| Work time | Half a day | Minutes |
| Context transmission | Every request | Only during planning |

## Technical Implementation Details

### System Prompt Changes
```
# Before:
You must use exactly one tool call per assistant response.

# After:
When planning multiple independent actions, return a list of tool calls to execute sequentially. 
The system will execute them and return all results together.
```

### Model Response Handling Changes
1. Allow the model to return an array of tool calls
2. Execute them sequentially (without parallelism initially)
3. Collect results into a unified context
4. Send all results in a single request

### Model Response Format
```json
{
  "tool_calls": [
    {"name": "read_file", "arguments": {"path": "colors.json"}},
    {"name": "browser_navigate", "arguments": {"url": "https://themes.vscode.one"}},
    {"name": "browser_snapshot", "arguments": {}}
  ]
}
```

## Alternatives

If full implementation is complex, consider starting with:

1. **Batch execution of similar tools** — allow multiple `read_file` or `browser_click` in a single response
2. **"Batch" mode** — optional flag to enable batch execution
3. **System instruction caching** — don't transmit them every time, use server-side cache

## Conclusion

The current Kilo Code architecture makes it economically inefficient for tasks requiring multiple actions. The proposed architectural change will:

- Reduce token consumption by 10-30x
- Decrease task execution time
- Make the tool more competitive

I request consideration of this PR and welcome alternative solutions if the proposed approach is not suitable.
author: nleononline

comments.author: github-actions
comments.body: This feature proposal relates to efficiency concerns documented in #3767, which also highlights the problem of inefficient API request patterns leading to excessive token consumption. Both issues address the need for more intelligent tool execution planning to reduce costs and improve performance.

body:
### Feature hasn't been suggested before.

- [x] I have verified this feature I'm about to request hasn't been suggested before.

### Describe the enhancement you want to request

Date/time: 2026-02-24T14:16:53.612Z
Extension version: 5.9.0
Provider: openai-codex
Model: gpt-5.2

Error reading file orchestrator/engine/transition_logic.py: Line with index 259 does not exist in '/home/saeed/repo/development-orchestrator-mcp/orchestrator/engine/transition_logic.py'. Note that line indexing is zero-based

---

**Request:**
The tool should be more helpful and provide hte number of lines in the error return.
author: basetutu

comments.author: github-actions
comments.body:
---
This issue might be a duplicate of existing issues. Please check:
- #5872: Error in calling parameters of read_file tool
- #4073: native tool call read_file tool error (can't read lines 1000-2000)
- #4679: Many LLMs use read_file to read directories, resulting in errors

These all relate to read_file tool issues and out-of-bounds errors. Before proceeding with implementation, please review whether this is a feature enhancement or related to one of these existing issues.

comments.author: basetutu
comments.body: Reviewed and all of the above are bugs and not a feature request.

comments.author: peterkc
comments.body:
The reported error message is specific to the v5.x extension architecture. The VSCode extension has migrated to v7.x which resolves this, but the JetBrains plugin remains affected.

## The error comes from the old architecture

The error message in the report:

```
Line with index 259 does not exist in '/path'. Note that line indexing is zero-based
```

is generated by `src/integrations/misc/read-lines.ts` (line 12), which only existed in the v5.x codebase:

```ts
// v5.x — src/integrations/misc/read-lines.ts
const outOfRangeError = (filepath: string, n: number) => {
  return new RangeError(
    `Line with index ${n} does not exist in '${filepath}'. Note that line indexing is zero-based`
  )
}
```

This error includes the requested line index but **not the total line count** — which is exactly the enhancement requested here.

## The v7.x rewrite already includes line counts

The v7.x release (Feb 23) replaced the entire tool implementation. The new `packages/opencode/src/tool/read.ts` (line 189–190) handles out-of-bounds differently:

```ts
// v7.x — packages/opencode/src/tool/read.ts
if (lines < offset && !(lines === 0 && offset === 1)) {
  throw new Error(`Offset ${offset} is out of range for this file (${lines} lines)`)
}
```

This already reports both the requested offset **and** the total line count.

## What changed between v5.x and v7.x

| Aspect | v5.x (`read-lines.ts`) | v7.x (`read.ts`) |
|--------|----------------------|------------------|
| Tool name | `read_file` | `read` |
| Parameters | `start_line` / `end_line` | `offset` / `limit` |
| Indexing | Zero-based | 1-based (commit `006d673`) |
| Error on OOB | Index only | Index + total line count |
| Architecture | `src/core/tools/ReadFileTool.ts` | `packages/opencode/src/tool/read.ts` |

The indexing was deliberately changed from 0-based to 1-based in [#13198](https://github.com/Kilo-Org/kilocode/pull/13198) to reduce confusion, since line numbers displayed to the user are 1-based.

## Platform status

| Platform | Latest version | Architecture | Bug status |
|----------|---------------|-------------|------------|
| VSCode | v7.0.33 | New (`packages/opencode/`) | Resolved |
| GoLand / IntelliJ / etc. | v5.9.0 | Old (`src/core/tools/`) | **Still affected** |

The old VSCode extension source now lives in [Kilo-Org/kilocode-legacy](https://github.com/Kilo-Org/kilocode-legacy), which is no longer actively maintained. VSCode users on v5.9.0 should auto-update to v7.x.

The [JetBrains plugin](https://plugins.jetbrains.com/plugin/28350-kilo-code) is still on v5.9.0 and bundles the old `extension.js` containing `read-lines.ts`. v7.x does not ship a JetBrains artifact, so this bug remains live for all JetBrains IDE users.

title: Extend i18n lint to cover all webview TSX files and fix remaining 22 hardcoded strings
body:
## Context

PR #XXX (feat: localize Agent Manager) introduced an AST-based lint test (`tests/unit/agent-manager-i18n.test.ts`) that uses `ts-morph` to detect hardcoded user-facing strings in TSX files. It currently covers only the Agent Manager files (`webview-ui/agent-manager/`), which are now fully localized with 0 violations.

A scan of all 42 webview TSX files shows **22 remaining hardcoded strings across 9 files**. The other 33 files are already clean.

## Remaining violations

| Count | File |
|---|---|
| 7 | `webview-ui/src/components/settings/AgentBehaviourTab.tsx` |
| 4 | `webview-ui/src/components/settings/AboutKiloCodeTab.tsx` |
| 3 | `webview-ui/src/components/chat/KiloNotifications.tsx` |
| 2 | `webview-ui/src/components/chat/PromptInput.tsx` |
| 2 | `webview-ui/src/components/settings/ProvidersTab.tsx` |
| 1 | `webview-ui/src/App.tsx` |
| 1 | `webview-ui/src/components/ProfileView.tsx` |
| 1 | `webview-ui/src/components/settings/ContextTab.tsx` |
| 1 | `webview-ui/src/components/settings/DisplayTab.tsx` |

## How to extend the lint

### Step 1: Expand `TSX_FILES` to cover all webview TSX

Replace the hardcoded file list with a glob:

```ts
import { globSync } from "node:fs"

const ROOT = path.resolve(import.meta.dir, "../..")
const TSX_FILES = globSync("webview-ui/**/*.tsx", { cwd: ROOT, absolute: false })
  .map(f => path.join(ROOT, f))
```

Alternatively, use `Bun.Glob` since the test runner is Bun.

### Step 2: Broaden the `isProgrammatic()` allowlist

The current filter is tuned for Agent Manager patterns (e.g. `am-` CSS prefixes, Agent Manager message types). The non-Agent-Manager files use different patterns that will produce false positives. You'll need to add:

- Additional CSS class prefixes used in other components
- Message type strings used in `postMessage()` calls (e.g. `requestProviders`, `providersLoaded`, etc.)
- VS Code API constants and config key strings
- URL strings (e.g. GitHub links in AboutKiloCodeTab)
- Known programmatic identifiers specific to settings tabs

The approach should be: run the expanded lint, triage each violation as either a genuine hardcoded string (needs `t()`) or a false positive (needs an `isProgrammatic` rule), then iterate until the test is green.

### Step 3: Fix the 22 genuine violations

For each hardcoded string:

1. Add the corresponding key to `webview-ui/src/i18n/en.ts`
2. Replace the hardcoded string with `t("key")` in the component
3. Add translations to all 15 non-English locale files (`ar`, `br`, `bs`, `da`, `de`, `es`, `fr`, `ja`, `ko`, `no`, `pl`, `ru`, `th`, `zh`, `zht`)

Most of these components already import and use `useLanguage()` — the strings were likely just missed during earlier localization passes.

### Step 4: Rename the test file

Since it will no longer be Agent-Manager-specific, rename from `agent-manager-i18n.test.ts` to something like `webview-i18n.test.ts` and update the `describe` block accordingly.

## Detection capabilities

The lint currently detects:
- **JSX text content** — raw English text between tags
- **String-literal JSX attributes** for `title`, `label`, `placeholder`, `aria-label`
- **`showToast` calls** with hardcoded `title` or `description`
- **Fallback patterns** — `|| "string"` (common for display text defaults)
- **Object literal properties** — `{ label: "string" }` or `{ title: "string" }`

This should catch the vast majority of user-facing strings without manual review.

title: Consider re-adding Architect mode (or enhancing Plan mode to write plans to /plans)
body:
## Summary

We should consider re-adding Architect mode, or at minimum enhancing Plan mode so it writes plan files as `.md` to a `/plans` directory in the project root. This was a native Kilo behavior that users adopted and now miss.

## Context from internal feedback

- Plan mode currently exists but **cannot write the plan as an `.md` file to `/plans`** in the user's project.
- Plan mode does persist plans, but they're hidden inside the `.opencode` directory — not easily accessible or visible to the user.
- The old Architect mode would always write the plan into the `/plans` folder when finished. Users adopted this workflow and notice it's gone.
- Architect as a separate mode isn't necessarily missed — what's missed is the **behavior** of persisting a readable plan file in the project.
- Suggestion: Allow Plan mode to both persist internally *and* create `.md` files in a `/plans` directory.
- Alternative: It's relatively easy to add a global custom mode, but having this as a built-in behavior is preferred.

## Proposed Options

1. **Enhance Plan mode** to also write `.md` plan files to a `/plans` directory in the project root (in addition to internal `.opencode` persistence).
2. **Re-add Architect mode** as a distinct mode that writes plans to `/plans`.
3. **Prompt Plan mode** to optionally create `.md` files in the plan directory when finishing a plan.

## References

- Internal Slack thread in #new-vscode-feedback
author: app/kilo-code-bot

comments.author: github-actions
comments.body:
This issue might be related to or a duplicate of existing discussions:
- #1284: "Architect mode" - An existing open issue about Architect mode functionality

Before proceeding, please review that issue to ensure this request isn't already being tracked there or if there's additional context that would help inform this feature request.

comments.author: shssoichiro
comments.body: My preference is that the plan file is hidden/temporary, but I can see the value of having it for some users' workflows. I think having both Plan and Architect mode would be confusing to users, if the only difference is where they write plan files. Perhaps the option to write plan files in `/plans` could be a user setting? Alternatively, having it as a prompted option.

title: perf: Markdown syntax highlighting blocks main thread for 2.3s+ during session switching
body:
## Problem

When switching sessions in the VS Code extension (Agent Manager), the webview main thread is blocked for **2.3+ seconds** by synchronous syntax highlighting in the Markdown rendering pipeline. During this time the UI is completely frozen — no scrolling, no clicking, no visual feedback.

## Profiling Data

Chrome DevTools Performance trace from the Agent Manager webview:

### Top-level breakdown

| Phase | Duration |
|---|---|
| `messageListener` (total postMessage handler) | 2,510ms |
| `handleMessagesLoaded` | 2,510ms |
| `setStore` (SolidJS store update + reactivity) | 2,455ms |

### CPU profile hot functions (sampled during the slow window)

| Function | Self Time | % of Total | Location |
|---|---|---|---|
| `findNextMatchSync` | 2,302ms | 91.4% | Oniguruma regex engine |
| `(garbage collector)` | — | 24.9% | GC pressure from string allocations |
| `assignProp` | — | 0.8% | SolidJS DOM updates |
| `_tokenizeString` | — | 0.1% | Shiki tokenizer |
| `handleCaptures` | — | 0.1% | TextMate grammar captures |
| `applyHunksToDOM` | — | 0.1% | Diff rendering |
| `tokensToHast` / `tokenizeWithTheme` | — | 0.1% | Shiki theme application |

### Bottom-up call tree (from trace)

```
findNextMatchSync     2,302ms  91.4%  (self time)
  matchRule           2,313ms  91.8%
    matchRuleOrInjections
      scanNext
        _tokenizeString
          _tokenize
            tokenizeLine2
              _tokenizeWithTheme
                tokenizeWithTheme
```

**`findNextMatchSync` (Oniguruma regex) is 91.4% of the entire message load time.** Everything else (HTTP fetch, JSON parse, IPC, SolidJS store update, DOM rendering) is under 200ms total.

### Session characteristics that trigger this

The profiled session had:
- 45 messages, 164 parts
- 1.49MB HTTP response payload
- Multiple assistant messages containing markdown with code blocks (tool outputs, code suggestions, file contents)
- The HTTP fetch + JSON parse takes only ~25ms — the rendering is the bottleneck

## Root Cause

The codebase has two syntax highlighting paths:

1. **Code/Diff components** (`packages/ui/src/pierre/worker.ts`) — uses a pool of 2 Web Workers with Oniguruma WASM. **Already off the main thread, not the problem.**

2. **Markdown code blocks** (`packages/ui/src/context/marked.tsx`) — uses the `marked-shiki` plugin which calls `highlighter.codeToHtml()` **synchronously on the main thread** during `marked.parse()`. **This is the bottleneck.**

When a session with 40+ messages loads, each assistant message contains markdown with code blocks. All code blocks across all messages are highlighted synchronously in one pass, blocking the main thread for seconds.

### Call Chain

```
handleMessagesLoaded()
  → setStore("messages", ...)      // SolidJS store update triggers reactivity
  → <Markdown text={...} />        // per-message component renders
  → marked.parse(markdown)         // marked parser (marked.tsx:540)
  → markedShiki.highlight(code)    // marked-shiki plugin (marked.tsx:512)
  → highlighter.codeToHtml(code)   // Shiki tokenization
  → findNextMatchSync()            // Oniguruma regex — BLOCKS MAIN THREAD
```

The `Markdown` component uses `createResource` (async), but `codeToHtml` internally calls `findNextMatchSync` which is synchronous CPU work — so even though the resource is async, it blocks the main thread while executing.

### Existing Mitigations (not sufficient)

- **200-entry LRU cache** in `Markdown` component (`markdown.tsx:14-15`) — skips re-parsing for repeated content. Helps on re-visits but not on first load.
- **100ms text throttle** (`createThrottledValue` in `message-part.tsx:116-147`) — only throttles streaming updates, not bulk loads.
- **Web Workers for Code/Diff** (`packages/ui/src/pierre/worker.ts`) — Code and Diff components already use worker pool with Oniguruma WASM. Markdown path doesn't use this.

## Fix Options

### Option A: Two-pass render — show plain text first, highlight async (Recommended)

Modify the `Markdown` component to do a two-pass render:

1. **First pass (instant):** Parse markdown but render code blocks as plain `<pre><code>` with no syntax highlighting. Messages appear immediately.
2. **Second pass (deferred):** Queue highlighting via `requestIdleCallback` or `setTimeout(0)` that updates the DOM after the initial render. Code blocks highlight progressively without blocking interaction.

Implementation approach:
- Add a `skipHighlighting` option to the `markedShiki` plugin that returns plain `<pre><code>` blocks
- On first render, use the skip mode
- Schedule the full highlight pass after the initial paint
- Replace plain blocks with highlighted HTML once ready

**Pros:** Instant perceived performance, no architectural changes, existing cache still works, brief flash of unhighlighted code is acceptable.
**Cons:** Slight visual flash as highlighting appears.

### Option B: Viewport-aware highlighting — only highlight visible messages

Use `IntersectionObserver` on message components to only highlight code blocks in messages currently visible in the viewport. Typically only 2-3 messages are visible at a time.

**Pros:** Minimal work on initial load.
**Cons:** More complex lifecycle management, highlighting pops in as user scrolls.

### Option C: Move markdown highlighting to a Web Worker

Extend the existing worker pool pattern to markdown:
- Send markdown text to a worker
- Worker runs `marked.parse()` + `codeToHtml()` with Oniguruma WASM
- Worker returns highlighted HTML string
- Main thread injects via `innerHTML` (DOMPurify sanitization still on main thread)

**Pros:** Completely unblocks main thread, consistent with existing Code/Diff architecture.
**Cons:** `marked-shiki` is tightly coupled to sync `marked` pipeline, requires HTML serialization across worker boundary.

### Option D: Verify JavaScript regex engine is used for main-thread highlighting

The code configures `preferredHighlighter: "shiki-js"` for the main thread (which uses `createJavaScriptRegexEngine()`). However, the trace shows `findNextMatchSync` (Oniguruma) being called. Investigate whether the `getSharedHighlighter` singleton was initialized with WASM before the JS fallback took effect, or if the engine selection isn't working as expected.

**Pros:** Potentially a simple configuration fix.
**Cons:** JS regex engine has slightly different tokenization for some grammars; may not fully solve the performance issue.

## Recommendation

**Option A** (two-pass render) provides the best UX with the least risk. It can be implemented entirely within:
- `packages/ui/src/components/markdown.tsx` — the Markdown component
- `packages/ui/src/context/marked.tsx` — the marked/Shiki configuration

**Option D** should be investigated first as a quick win — if Oniguruma is being used on the main thread when it should be using the JS regex engine, fixing that alone could significantly reduce highlighting time.

## Relevant Files

| File | Purpose |
|---|---|
| `packages/ui/src/context/marked.tsx` | Shiki highlighter setup for markdown code blocks (main thread). Lines 426-460 (post-parse highlighting), lines 511-526 (marked-shiki plugin) |
| `packages/ui/src/components/markdown.tsx` | `Markdown` component. Calls `marked.parse()`, sanitizes with DOMPurify, diffs DOM with morphdom. 200-entry LRU cache |
| `packages/ui/src/components/message-part.tsx` | Message part rendering. `TextPart` (line 670) renders `<Markdown>` |
| `packages/ui/src/pierre/worker.ts` | Web Worker pool setup (reference for worker-based highlighting pattern) |

## Versions

- Shiki: 3.20.0
- marked-shiki: 1.2.1
- @pierre/diffs: 1.1.0-beta.13
- @shikijs/transformers: 3.9.2

title: 供应商错误：未知api错误
body:
### Description

Date/time: 2026-02-24T08:07:52.395Z
Extension version: 5.9.0
Provider: openai-responses (proxy)
Model: gpt-5.3-codex

Unexpected API Response: The language model did not provide any assistant messages. This may indicate an issue with the API or the model's output.
看起來你在使用基於代理的提供商。請檢查日誌並確保它不會重寫 Roo 的請求。

---------------------------------

使用的是OpenAI服务中间商

---------------------------------

把kilo-diagnostics-.json文件丢给kilo，kilo给出的解决方案：

根因在这段报错本身就已经暴露出来了：模型返回里没有任何 assistant message，平台侧在解析时直接抛错。

结合你贴的完整 history，最可能是下面这个触发链：

在 code 模式里，assistant 先连续用了工具（update_todo_list、read_file、write_to_file...）。
然后有一次 assistant 直接发了普通文本总结（“已把应用改造成……”），没有工具调用。
你的运行环境立刻回了系统错误提示：[ERROR] You did not use a tool in your previous response! Please retry with a tool use.
这说明该回合被编排器判定为“非法回合”（在该模式/状态下必须 tool_use），后续很容易导致上游 openai-responses 聚合不到有效 assistant 消息，最终出现：
Unexpected API Response: The language model did not provide any assistant messages.
简化一句话：

不是你业务代码报错，而是代理编排协议被打断（该回合期待 tool_use，却收到了纯文本），导致响应解析器拿不到合法 assistant 消息。

你可以这样修：

在受控代理流程里，每一回合都遵守当前模式约束（比如 code 模式要求先 tool_use）。
完成任务时用平台规定的结束工具（你这套里是 attempt_completion），不要直接发自由文本收尾。
如果上一条是工具调用，先等对应 tool_result 再继续；避免“工具链未闭环”。
对“自动提醒消息”（例如你看到的 [ERROR] You did not use a tool...）必须按指令走下一次 tool_use，不要先解释性文本。
如果你愿意，我可以再给你一版“从这份日志自动检测此类协议错误”的检查清单（几条规则就能在本地快速定位）。

### Plugins

kilo for vs code

### Kilo version

5.9

### Steps to reproduce

1.让ai帮忙写代码
2.上下文跑到29k左右报错

### Screenshot and/or share link

_No response_

### Operating System

Windows 11

### Terminal

VS Code
